data.title: CGDV: Great Fund To Own For Income And Gains - BUY
data.description:
Introduction

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV) is a fund that seeks to give investors consistent income that exceeds the average yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on companies with dividends currently or those which have the potential to pay dividends. They also hold some growth oriented names, where investors can have some upside exposure to growth names. Below are some details of the ETF taken from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.33%

  • Fund Assets: $20.4B

  • Volume: 3.8M

Their top holdings and exposure by sector and region are shown below.

We like the top holdings of the fund as we will discuss in the following section.

Top Holdings Review

Microsoft (MSFT)

This is a leading enterprise AI player and they recently reported strong earnings - revenue was up 18% on a year over year basis. The firm also stated that their cloud business produced over $75B in the 2025 fiscal year, which was up 34% from the prior year. We are pleased to see the enterprise computing business of MSFT grow and we believe that they are well positioned to continue being leaders in the enterprise AI space. Further, on the consumer side, we believe that as Windows 11 continues its takeover of the broader market as Windows 10 runs into its end of life this fall, there will be a large upgrade cycle that takes place in personal PCs as users shift their systems to the latest operating software. This should help to boost MSFT’s other business as well, in addition to their enterprise computing side of the house. The following image shows the growth of all of their businesses - and it's clear that not only is MSFT diversified, they are growing across the firm.

We are happy to see MSFT as the top holding of this fund for their growth, diversity of revenues, and strong future ambitions.

Broadcom (AVGO)

AVGO is a leading custom AI chip manufacturer, and they have big name partnerships with firms such as Google. We believe that AVGO represents a strong way to play the AI investment trend, as it allows investors to gain access to more than just standard GPUs that are used in data centers. Further, AVGO is consistently innovating in its products - as revealed in this press release from earlier this week. They are shipping their new Jericho4 ethernet fabric router, which is purpose built for the AI data center industry, to connect multiple data centers at once. They are building a strong portfolio of products that can perform in both high performance computing and AI use cases, and as a result, we view that AVGO remains a strong player in this field and one that is very worthy of investment. Their chips will allow data center operators to spread the power and usage requirements across more than one physical space, and this is where AVGO’s latest products come in. With the ability to connect numerous data centers across different regions, there will be an opportunity to increase data loads even further, leading to greater performance and heavier traffic loads for clients. This is ultimately a very strong positive for AVGO, and we believe that investors would be wise to look into this firm as an option for investing into the AI space in the future. Their products are critical to any data center and they have a diverse range of product options for many use cases.

Nvidia (NVDA)

As data centers continue to grow, there will be more and more need for AI chips to power the training and inference workloads that come through. NVDA products are leaders in this industry and they are led by a great CEO in Jensen Huang. As big tech firms have already spent $155B this year on AI capex alone, with more to go as forecasted, there is a large chunk of the pie that will go to NVDA. This trend, we believe, will continue for the foreseeable future due to the insatiable demand for AI computing that is trending now across all industries. As AMD CEO Lisa Su has commented regarding the increasing total addressable market for AI chips, this trend is going to lift all boats in the AI ocean but certainty will benefit NVDA as they are the largest player. We view NVDA as a pure play AI investment name, and despite having competition from the other chip players, they are still poised to benefit from the waves of AI investment that is coming. Even in China, as the chip restriction was recently lifted last month from the Trump administration, there has been demand for NVDA products, and this demand has been reflected in higher orders for the H20 chip from the manufacturer, Taiwan Semiconductor. NVDA is in a good spot to benefit from this AI investment cycle and they should remain leaders in the space. We are pleased to see them as a top holding in this ETF.

Fund Strategy

As Capital Group puts it, this fund “Seeks to produce consistent income that exceeds the average yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on companies that pay dividends or have the potential to pay dividends.” They go on to state “The fund's investment objectives are to produce income exceeding the average yield on U.S. stocks generally and to provide an opportunity for growth of principal consistent with sound common stock investing.”

Unpacking this, we see 3 major goals of the fund, in rough order of importance:

  1. Exceed the average yield of the S&P 500

  2. Invest in dividend paying companies (or companies that will likely pay dividends)

  3. Invest in companies that will grow in value

Let’s see how well the fund has met these goals.

Exceed the average yield of the S&P 500

The yield of CGDV is currently at 1.42% which is indeed higher than the S&P’s current yield of 1.22%. While it is true that the fund currently has a higher yield, it is not higher by much. On a $1000 investment, the difference equates to $2 per year. Historically, the yield of CGDV vs the S&P 500 are shown below (note: we use VOO as a proxy for the S&P 500 here).

We see that for much of the fund’s existence it has had a yield that was at or below that of the S&P 500.

Invest in dividend paying companies (or companies that will likely pay dividends)

We look at the top 10 holdings of CGDV and their respective dividend yields. For all of the companies, we source the TTM dividend yield from Seeking Alpha

We see that the top 10 holdings have an average yield of 1.42% and a weighted average yield of 1.33%. We see that their goal of investing in dividend paying companies was technically met, but as discussed earlier, the yields are lackluster with the top 10 holdings only yielding 0.11% more than the S&P.

Invest in companies that will grow in value

To assess this, we look at the price return of the fund compared to the S&P 500. Note: We are looking at price return here not total return so dividends are excluded.

We see that CGDV has indeed had a higher price return than the S&P 500 over the last 3 years with a total gain of 73.61% compared to the S&P’s 51.96%.

Risks

AI Investment trend rotates elsewhere

There is the possibility that investors move away from AI firms and high flying computing names, and instead move capital into a more stable sector. Despite being a lower risk, we view that this should still be brought into consideration if the broader markets and economy suffer at the hands of poor macroeconomic news.

High Cost

The fund has an expense ratio of 0.33% compared to VOO for example that has an expense ratio of 0.03%. While it is common for an actively managed fund to have a higher expense ratio, we should keep in mind that the major goals of the fund are to beat the S&P 500 in both growth and dividends. Considering that the dividend yield of the fund is only 0.2% higher than the S&P’s yield, the 0.3% difference in expense ratio doesn’t seem as justifiable. Of course, this is ignoring the overall price return of the fund which has clearly been better than the S&Ps return over the last 3 years.

Conclusion

We are certainly big fans of the top holdings of this fund. They represent some of the best leaders in the big tech and AI space, and should have room to grow in the future as they are continuously innovating on their product portfolios and latest developments in AI.

We see that the fund has met the major investment goals that were laid forth in the prospectus which is great. We don’t love that the dividend yield on a “dividend value ETF” is not much higher than the S&Ps dividend, but are pleased with the overall performance of the fund when you factor in price growth.

We rate CGDV as a buy.

data.title: The London Company SMID Cap Vs. Russell 2500 Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

After the significant downturn in the first quarter, U.S. equities experienced a double-digit increase in 2Q. The broader market, as measured by the Russell 3000 Index, increased 11.0%. Volatility surged in early April following new U.S. tariff announcements aimed at reshaping global trade. A double-digit, risk-on rally ensued following a temporary pause in tariff escalations, widespread optimism around enterprise AI, and a healthy earnings outlook. Additionally, improving sentiment, a healthy labor market, and expectations of future Federal Reserve rate cuts further supported the upward momentum, even amidst geopolitical tensions and mixed economic signals. Growth styles led Value, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Turning to market factors, Growth and Volatility posted the strongest returns, and most Momentum factors outperformed. Value and Yield factors had a negative impact, while Quality factors were mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company SMID portfolio decreased 0.6% (-0.8% net) during the second quarter vs. an 8.6% increase in the Russell 2500 Index. Sector allocation was a tailwind to relative performance, offset by stock allocation.

  • The SMID portfolio came up short of our 85-90% upside capture expectations in 2Q. Our exposure to Lower Volatility and Quality factors flipped from a tailwind in 1Q to a headwind in 2Q, as high beta and momentum driven rallied sharply following tariff de-escalation. Pockets of weakness across several holdings further exacerbated relative performance headwinds amidst the risk-on rally. We elected to maintain our position in a few of the weaker positions, reflecting our long-term conviction in the businesses. Fortunes can change quickly with a high conviction portfolio, and we remain confident in the operating metrics of portfolio companies. While the Index is broader down cap, the absence of owning the top performers contributed to nearly 20% of the underperformance this quarter. The recent strength of some of the riskier, more speculative Mid & Small Caps names is not driven by fundamentals nor is it sustainable longer-term.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • NewMarket Corporation (NEU) – NEU outperformed this quarter, fueled by strong AMPAC revenue growth from better volume and product mix, despite weaker Petroleum Additives results. The planned capacity expansion for the AMPAC business signals robust demand for rocket additives in a stable, oligopolistic market. Management prioritizes debt reduction and shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.

  • Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) – AWI shares outperformed in the quarter due to beating expectations, driven by increased volumes from recent acquisitions and an increase in pricing. We continue to like AWI for its consistent execution, strong financials, leading market share and persistent moats through its exclusivity agreements and warranties.

  • AerCap Holdings (AER) – AER delivered strong performance this quarter, bolstered by solid Q1 2025 results and a favorable UK court ruling on $1B in insurance claims related to the Russia-Ukraine War. With the largest aircraft portfolio in a seller’s market, AerCap is well-positioned to grow book value per share through strategic asset sales and stock buybacks, capitalizing on robust market dynamics.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Murphy USA, Inc. (MUSA) – MUSA was a bottom performer after reporting softer market share gains in Q1 due to lower retail fuel prices. With that said, the long-term components of our thesis remain intact: structurally higher industry fuel margins, everyday low price driving profitable market share gains, and value-creating capital allocation.

  • Saia, Inc. (SAIA) – SAIA remained weak this quarter as the lingering “freight recession” drags on for almost its third year. We remain convicted in our ownership of SAIA due to its solid balance sheet and strong network growth plan. We believe the company could be a multi-year compounder, assuming the freight industry returns to normal demand patterns.

  • White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd (WTM) – WTM was a bottom name after facing industry headwinds. For U.S. P&C insurers, performance was mundane in 2Q, whereas the rest of the market had a rebound. We remain confident in the company’s ability to deliver outsized growth in book value per share over time through prudent capital allocation.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight in both Energy and Real Estate (weaker performing sectors)

  • What Hurt: Overweight in Consumer Staples and Materials (weaker performing sectors)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Exited: Cannae Holdings, Inc. (CNNE) – Underperformed in recent years and was only 1% of the portfolio. Our initial thesis centered on Bill Foley’s ability to create value and the most valuable asset of CNNE was Dun & Bradstreet. That investment has not worked out and CNNE shares have lagged the broader market. We elected to sell the remaining position.

  • Reduced: AerCap Holdings – We trimmed AER on strength. The stock has significantly outperformed the broader market since early 2021. We remain confident in the outlook for air travel and for AER to benefit by leasing planes to the largest airlines.

  • Increased: Saia, Inc. – With recent weakness and a smaller beginning weight in the portfolio, we felt it was prudent to add to the shares and make it a 3% position.

  • Increased: Zebra Technologies Corporation (ZBRA) – Added to the position following weakness in the shares early in the year. Concerns around tariffs and weaker tech spending provided a more attractive valuation.

  • Initiated: Somnigroup International Inc (SGI) – Formerly Tempur Sealy, SGI is the combined company of Tempur Sealy the acquired Mattress Firm. SGI is the clear leader in the bedding industry, and the company is gaining market share while generating robust free cash flow. Strong brand equity, solid management execution, and vertical integration from the acquisition support our investment thesis. Currently, the bedding market is weak with volumes near a 30-year low. Higher mortgage rates and rising home prices may limit unit sales in the short term. Longer term, we believe that an eventual industry rebound, synergies from the acquisition, and greater innovation are keys to the company’s success. We have owned SGI in the past and just sold it from the Small Cap portfolio due to its rising market cap following the acquisition of Mattress Firm.

Looking Ahead

There is elevated uncertainty as we start the second half of 2025 with a high likelihood of greater tariffs being announced in the weeks ahead. Consumer confidence has declined recently due to the risks of additional tariffs, but the broader economic data still supports growth in the near term, although growth is decelerating. While we are not predicting a recession, the odds of a recession have increased. In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears to be on a steady course for two or three rate cuts later in the year. The effect of tariffs on both inflation and the broader economy could change those plans, though.

In terms of the equity market, the S&P 500 is back to the expensive, concentrated territory in which it began the year. Meanwhile, the macro risk backdrop has deteriorated since the start of 2025, including labor market softening, ongoing tariff uncertainty, debt headwinds, geopolitical tensions, and unclear inflation trends. Equity returns in the near term may be modest, with shareholder yield (dividends, share repurchases, debt reduction) comprising a significant percentage of the total return from equities. We believe our high-Quality, low-Volatility orientation positions us well for an environment of elevated policy risks and fragile global growth. We remain rooted to our long-term, fundamental investment approach, focusing on company quality, sustainable returns on capital, and resilience across economic scenarios.

data.title: Ormat Technologies: Geothermal Is Getting Overdue Attention
data.description:
Ormat Technologies overview

Ormat Technologies (NYSE:ORA) is the leading geothermal energy developer and operator in the US. Despite the tiny share of energy production that comes from geothermal, the recent legislation (OBBBA) recognizes the potential and maintains incentives while cutting them for solar, wind and other transition technologies.

The main attractiveness of geothermal (apart from no emissions) is its capacity to produce base-load power, something that matches well with the expected growth in data center demand, which is 24/7. It is also dramatically more acceptable to nearby communities than nuclear, and without the spent fuel dilemma.

The combination of both, the need to shift toward cleaner power generation and the step up in electricity demand growth that will come from underlying trends (AI, automation, etc.) makes geothermal a key technology going forward.

Moreover, advances in rock drilling and water injection from the oil and gas sector are beginning to be applied for geothermal, vastly expanding potential development sites.

Ormat thesis:

I have a buy rating and long-term long thesis on Ormat. Initially covered a few months ago arguing the company was “having its moment” but that was prior to the OBBBA legislation. So, we could say that “NOW the company is having its moment”.

As discussed in the linked article, the company was then trading near replacement value of their installed base, with positive growth pipeline and growing EBITDA margins. The situation has not changed materially, despite the stock price growing by some 12%, the overall valuation is still attractive given the potential for more utility interest at the state level and tax support at the federal level.

In addition to developing, owning and operating generation assets, ORMAT is also a supplier of key technology to others. The big “wild card” upside on geothermal is achieving “next generation” technology advancements. The basic technology as it stands today is similar to what it was decades ago. But new techniques, developed within the oil and gas (and fracking) industries may transform this industry as well.

EGS:

Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS), not to be confused with ESG, is potentially a game changer. That is because current potential is centered around relatively few sites where trapped steam (the resource) is close enough to the surface and large enough to merit drilling for production.

EGS takes advances in materials and techniques from fracking to develop geothermal heat transfer by drilling into hot rock formations and using a fluid (CO2 is a potential one) for heat transfer.

Should these techniques proved economical, the potential sites grow exponentially, in the US and all over the world. That would be a major boon to Ormat, perhaps the only public company focused almost exclusively on geothermal.

With EGS progress firmly in mind, the company recently announced a couple of key hires, one (Daniel Moelk) comes with extensive EGS background and as SVP of resource, drilling and EGS will lead the company’s efforts in bringing the technology closer to commercial reality.

The main challenge is cost. But cost is a relative thing. Competing with a combined-cycle natural gas plant on capex was impossible, and is still out of reach, assuming no cost for emissions, but the gap has closed considerably given increasing demand for base load power (for which only nuclear and natural gas are acceptable now).

Geothermal is more expensive but requires no fuel. At a levelized cost the comparative cost of the technologies is getting increasingly closer, even if the upfront costs are still higher for geothermal, the cost difference (not to mention the seven-year wait times for turbines) may sway some utilities toward favoring geothermal (and nuclear) vs. the traditional base load go-to of CCGT.

The long-term thesis was strengthened by OBBB legislation. States have their own decarbonization targets and now federal incentives favor it vs the other more widespread renewables. The impacts of this will take some years to show up in the numbers, but let's look at how the company is doing so far this year.

Ormat Technologies second quarter earnings:

The company reported Q2 earnings this week and re-affirmed guidance for 2025, including EBITDA of just shy of $600 million, which shows ample cash generation and debt coverage. Earnings per share accelerated in the second quarter and remain 12% better on a YTD basis vs prior year:

Although quarter by quarter earnings are not too important; the company has set a target for $800 million EBITDA by 2028. Progress toward that goal is important to keep an eye on, as it depends on project execution pipeline going according to plan.

Further, the $600 million in EBITDA expected for 2025 implies a 4x gearing on consolidated debt of $2.5B (a portion is project-level and non-recourse). This is normal gearing for the mix of assets but highlights the importance of EBITDA growth toward planned target.

ORA stock valuation:

The unsustainable multiples that followed the green energy boom have been largely reduced to still-lofty but more sustainable levels. One thing to consider when looking at earnings is that the generation portfolio is undergoing PPA renewals.

Offtakers, be it large end users or utilities (some of them in exotic international locations) are very likely to experience higher renewal pricing, for their new term contracts, given the before mentioned energy supply glut that most see coming - especially in the US.

But the real upside is in growing the generation portfolio at established sites and, when possible, at scale using EGS.

On the prior article, linked at the top, I discussed valuation much more fully and not much has changed in the past few months. There, I saw a path toward $7B market cap, given management estimates on 3 year forward EBITDA (solidly based on ongoing expansions) and conservative multiples for the industry.

After the recent rally, the upside has been reduced to 40%, but recent events have also removed uncertainty - and perhaps warrant a higher EV/EBITDA multiple than the 12x I used.

Risk:

EGS may not reach scalable commercial deployment. The main problems seem to have been solved. After all the process involves injecting water into hot rock and then recovering or running a fluid through the "artificial reserve". It sounds a lot like fracking, right?

Still, the technology needs to be proven (either by Ormat or others) in actual demonstration plants for any real traction to persist.

Government incentives everywhere are crucial for developing new technologies, and they compete with many other potential uses so are never guaranteed. The next few years will be important to see if geothermal will remain a tiny part of the solution or could perhaps become a more relevant factor.

Debt is not a problem for ORA, their contracted energy output ensures a steady revenue stream no matter the economic conditions. But levels and coverage ratios need to be watched if any significant greenfield projects or M&A comes through.

International power projects expose the company to both FX risks and political risks. In many countries, the underground resource is a state good and exploitation needs to be negotiated (not unlike minear resources) but often lacks any regulatory framework. This is another challenge to growing outside developed nations but where the potential is greatest, so is the installed capacity. Offshore jurisdictions vary from New Zealand, Indonesia and Kenya to Central America.

Summary:

Geothermal is the only source of energy that does not (directly or indirectly) come from the sun. Fossil fuels come from long-gone carbon-based life, which depended on sunlight. Hydro requires water's potential energy at elevation, only possible by ocean evaporation.

The heat from the core of the Earth is constant, it is always "on" and can therefore produce base power unlike all other renewables. Yet we have only partially been able to harness it.

New technologies and applications from the oil and gas sector have shown the way to greatly expand our potential to tap into this endless source of energy - and administrators in D.C. have recognized the potential by maintaining incentives for geothermal, next generation geothermal and storage solutions (all areas where Ormat has a finger).

data.title: The London Company Mid Cap Vs. RMC Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

After the significant downturn in the first quarter, U.S. equities experienced a double-digit increase in 2Q. The broader market, as measured by the Russell 3000 Index, increased 11.0%. Volatility surged in early April following new U.S. tariff announcements aimed at reshaping global trade. A double-digit, risk-on rally ensued following a temporary pause in tariff escalations, widespread optimism around enterprise AI, and a healthy earnings outlook. Additionally, improving sentiment, a healthy labor market, and expectations of future Federal Reserve rate cuts further supported the upward momentum, even amidst geopolitical tensions and mixed economic signals. Growth styles led Value, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Turning to market factors, Growth and Volatility posted the strongest returns, and most Momentum factors outperformed. Value and Yield factors had a negative impact, while Quality factors were mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company Mid Cap portfolio increased 5.7% during the second quarter (5.5% net) vs. an 8.5% increase in the Russell Midcap Index. Both sector allocation and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance.

  • The Mid Cap portfolio produced solid absolute returns in 2Q, but relative results came up short of our 85-90% upside capture expectations. We received limited benefit from our Quality orientation, and our structural underexposure to high-momentum names was a material headwind. While the Index is broader down cap, the absence of owning the Top 10 performers in the Russell Midcap contributed to over 40% of the underperformance this quarter. Pockets of weakness across several holdings further exacerbated relative performance headwinds amidst the risk-on rally. We elected to maintain our position in a few of the weaker positions, reflecting our long-term conviction in the businesses. The recent strength of some of the riskier, more speculative Mid Cap names is not driven by fundamentals nor is it sustainable longer-term. Fortunes can change quickly with a high conviction portfolio, and we remain confident in the operating metrics of portfolio companies.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Amphenol Corporation (APH) – APH was a top name as it is a beneficiary of AI spending, driven by surging demand for its electronic connectors, interconnect systems, antennas, and sensors used in AI datacenters. As such, the stock has been very strong as of late. We remain positive on Amphenol’s diversified business model, market leadership, and robust balance sheet, which positions it for sustained growth.

  • Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR) – DLTR was a top performer after it reached an agreement in March to sell its underperforming Family Dollar business, a decade-long drag on growth and margins. This divestiture has driven a stock re-rating, reflecting a higher-quality company focused on value creation at the Dollar Tree banner. The recent elimination of the de minimis exemption has yielded positive sentiment towards retailers like DLTR. We remain attracted to its pricing flexibility and margin enhancement opportunity.

  • NewMarket Corporation (NEU) – NEU outperformed this quarter, fueled by strong AMPAC revenue growth from better volume and product mix, despite weaker Petroleum Additives results. The planned capacity expansion for the AMPAC business signals robust demand for rocket additives in a stable, oligopolistic market. Management prioritizes debt reduction and shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Copart, Inc. (CPRT) – CPRT was a bottom performer after reporting softer earnings results with elevated expectations. CPRT has been a top holding in 5 of the last 10 years since holding it in Mid Cap. It has leading market share in all its markets and continues to expand its moat with capacity expansions. The fundamentals and trends remain strong for the business.

  • Entegris, Inc. (ENTG) – ENTG continues to be weak due to challenges from underutilized mainstream fabs, less visibility in older technology, and weaker capex-driven demand. ENTG’s solutions for advanced technology and incremental wafer content gains continue to trend upward. ENTG is one of the most diversified players in the semi-materials industry, with its size and scale. We remain attracted to the industry’s high barriers to entry, limited competitors, and high switching costs.

  • Churchill Downs Incorporated (CHDN) – CHDN has underperformed due to weakness in regional gaming, concern about consumer spending, and lower Derby profitability y/y. We note that the Derby was facing the 150th anniversary last year, and we continue to like CHDN, its highly cash-generative assets, track record of good capital allocation, and opportunities to reinvest in the business at attractive returns.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight in Energy (a weaker performing sector) and overweight in Industrials (a better performing sector)

  • What Hurt: Underweight in Information Technology (a better performing sector) and overweight in Consumer Staples (a weaker performing sector)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Increased: Dollar Tree, Inc. – Added to DLTR following the sale of Family Dollar. Shares of DLTR were very weak in 2023 and 2024 as the company struggled to turn around and/or find a buyer for Family Dollar. Post the Family Dollar sale, we believe DLTR margins could improve while the stock trades at only 9x EV/EBITDA. DLTR will also have a large tax loss carryforward to reduce future taxes.

  • Reduced: Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) – Trim followed strength in the shares and our desire to manage the position size.

  • Reduced: Post Holdings, Inc. (POST) – Trim followed strength in the shares and our desire to manage the position size.

  • Exited: Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) – Sold remaining position reflecting greater competition, client concentration, and recent management changes. We first purchased SWKS in late 2018. Shares of SWKS did well in the early years of our holding period, but the stock underperformed the broader market in recent years. While it is frustrating to sell on weakness, the recent loss of exclusivity on an iPhone 17 component with Apple highlights a significant risk to the firm. Apple represents over 60% of revenue to SWKS, so any change in the relationship is meaningful. This is the second loss of a component used in iPhones in recent years and is a sign of greater competition from large players like Qualcomm and Broadcom. SWKS also recently fired their CEO, which is another risk in the future. We decided to sell based on these concerns.

  • Increased: Churchill Downs Incorporated – Added to the existing position reflecting confidence in the long-term growth story, recent insider purchases, and an attractive valuation (less than 12x EBITDA).

Looking Ahead

There is elevated uncertainty as we start the second half of 2025 with a high likelihood of greater tariffs being announced in the weeks ahead. Consumer confidence has declined recently due to the risks of additional tariffs, but the broader economic data still supports growth in the near term, although growth is decelerating. While we are not predicting a recession, the odds of a recession have increased. In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears to be on a steady course for two or three rate cuts later in the year. The effect of tariffs on both inflation and the broader economy could change those plans, though.

In terms of the equity market, the S&P 500 is back to the expensive, concentrated territory in which it began the year. Meanwhile, the macro risk backdrop has deteriorated since the start of 2025, including labor market softening, ongoing tariff uncertainty, debt headwinds, geopolitical tensions, and unclear inflation trends. Equity returns in the near term may be modest, with shareholder yield (dividends, share repurchases, debt reduction) comprising a significant percentage of the total return from equities. We believe our high-Quality, low-Volatility orientation positions us well for an environment of elevated policy risks and fragile global growth. We remain rooted to our long-term, fundamental investment approach, focusing on company quality, sustainable returns on capital, and resilience across economic scenarios.

data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

After the significant downturn in the first quarter, U.S. equities experienced a double-digit increase in 2Q. The broader market, as measured by the Russell 3000 Index, increased 11.0%. Volatility surged in early April following new U.S. tariff announcements aimed at reshaping global trade. A double-digit, risk-on rally ensued following a temporary pause in tariff escalations, widespread optimism around enterprise AI, and a healthy earnings outlook. Additionally, improving sentiment, a healthy labor market, and expectations of future Federal Reserve rate cuts further supported the upward momentum, even amidst geopolitical tensions and mixed economic signals. Growth styles led Value, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Turning to market factors, Growth and Volatility posted the strongest returns, and most Momentum factors outperformed. Value and Yield factors had a negative impact, while Quality factors were mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company Income Equity portfolio returned 4.7% (4.5% net) during the first quarter vs. a 3.8% gain in the Russell 1000 Value Index. Sector allocation was a tailwind to relative performance, slightly offset by stock selection.

  • The Income Equity portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000 Value in 2Q, and exceeded our expectations of 85-90% upside capture. Our exposure to Yield, Lower Volatility & Quality factors flipped from a tailwind in 1Q to a headwind in 2Q, as high beta and momentum factors rallied sharply following tariff de-escalation. Fortunately, the portfolio was able to overcome these obstacles due to sector exposure tailwinds and stock selection, particularly among its Cons. Staples & Comm. Services holdings.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Nintendo Co., Ltd. ADR (OTCPK:NTDOY) – NTDOY was a top performer driven by excitement surrounding the Switch 2 launch and pre-order demand ahead of management’s base case. This positive momentum aligns with our long-term thesis, reinforcing our confidence in the company’s potential for a highly successful and profitable console cycle.

  • Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) – MSFT was a strong contributor during the quarter due to its leading position in AI infrastructure and applications. While the company’s partnership with OpenAI has become tense of late, both parties remain industry leaders and have strong incentives to work together. We like that MSFT’s operating system and productivity software businesses are entrenched with users, and that AI provides strong optionality for the franchise.

  • Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) – PM shares outperformed due to strong execution and an improving outlook. The primary driver remains the success of its smoke-free products, with sustained momentum in IQOS and ZYN, supported by robust pricing in the combustibles portfolio. We believe the combination of smoke-free growth potential and a resilient combustibles business will generate significant and sustainable free cash flow in the years ahead.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Apple Inc. (AAPL) – AAPL was left behind its Mag 7 brethren during the quarter, as investors debate the company’s apparent missteps in deploying AI solutions across its extensive user base. Its unmatched global user base plus ecosystem, top-tier talent, cash-rich balance sheet, and robust capital return program reinforce our confidence in AAPL’s ability to innovate and sustain its competitive moat.

  • Progressive Corporation (PGR) – PGR was a bottom performer this quarter due to investor concerns over decelerating policy and premium growth, despite strong margins and robust policy expansion driven by its leadership in personal auto insurance. We remain attracted to its best-in-class operations, conservative underwriting, and shareholder-friendly capital allocation philosophy.

  • Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) – APD was a bottom contributor this quarter as the new leadership addressed past mismanagement in clean energy projects, impacting investor sentiment. The business is now on a path towards de-risking and returning to a higher structural level of profitability. We were encouraged to hear that free cash flow losses are expected to end after this year, and a sharper capital allocation policy is being implemented. The strength and stability of the core industrial gas business remain unchanged.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight in Health Care (a weaker performing sector) and overweight in Information Technology (a better performing sector)

  • What Hurt: Overweight position in Consumer Staples (a weaker performing sector) and underweight in Industrials (a better performing sector)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Initiated: Chubb Limited (CB) – CB engages in the provision of commercial & personal property and casualty insurance, personal accident and accident & health (A&H), reinsurance, and life insurance. Roughly 2/3 of its profits are generated in the U.S. with high-growth Asian markets representing another 20% of earnings. CB has a portfolio of top-performing, multibillion-dollar businesses that have substantial scale and yet potential for growth. CB has a culture of superior underwriting discipline and management has a strong track record of expense control. CB also has a well-balanced mix of business by customer and product, with extensive distribution channels. We believe CB offers investors a rare combination of consistent growth, disciplined underwriting, geographical diversification into high-growth markets, and a well-managed investment portfolio, making it a compelling long-term hold.

  • Increased: Chevron Corporation (CVX), Dominion Energy Inc (D), Corning Inc (GLW), & Norfolk Southern Corporation (NSC) – Added to the existing positions following recent weakness in the shares. There’s no change in the long-term thesis for the businesses.

  • Reduced: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B (BRK.B) & Philip Morris International Inc. – Trimmed both positions on strength to manage valuation and risks in the portfolio. No change in the long-term thesis for the businesses.

  • Exited: Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) – Sold remaining position due to greater competition from fixed wireless and pressure on the consumer. While we hoped to see this slow as the market reached saturation, pressures on the consumer have only intensified competition.

  • Exited: United Parcel Service, Inc. Class B (UPS) – Sale reflects concerns over a challenging macroeconomic environment combined with the loss of a large customer. This creates a problematic execution story that we believe will be increasingly difficult for UPS to balance. Once we get through this transition period or the economic backdrop clears, we would consider going back into the investment.

Looking Ahead

There is elevated uncertainty as we start the second half of 2025 with a high likelihood of greater tariffs being announced in the weeks ahead. Consumer confidence has declined recently due to the risks of additional tariffs, but the broader economic data still supports growth in the near term, although growth is decelerating. While we are not predicting a recession, the odds of a recession have increased. In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears to be on a steady course for two or three rate cuts later in the year. The effect of tariffs on both inflation and the broader economy could change those plans, though.

In terms of the equity market, the S&P 500 is back to the expensive, concentrated territory in which it began the year. Meanwhile, the macro risk backdrop has deteriorated since the start of 2025, including labor market softening, ongoing tariff uncertainty, debt headwinds, geopolitical tensions, and unclear inflation trends. Equity returns in the near term may be modest, with shareholder yield (dividends, share repurchases, debt reduction) comprising a significant percentage of the total return from equities. We believe our high-Quality, low-Volatility orientation positions us well for an environment of elevated policy risks and fragile global growth. We remain rooted to our long-term, fundamental investment approach, focusing on company quality, sustainable returns on capital, and resilience across economic scenarios.

data.title: The London Company International Equity Vs. MSCI EAFE Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

Global equity markets rallied and broadened in 2Q, with strong gains across developed and emerging markets. The quarter started with volatility following the April 2nd “Liberation Day” tariff announcement by the U.S. administration. Global markets sharply plunged but quickly reversed course as deadlines were pushed out to allow for negotiation avoiding the worst-case outcome. Trends established in 1Q continued, rising global equity markets and a weaker U.S. Dollar—bolstering international market returns. In developed markets, MSCI World index gained 11.5%. International markets outpaced the U.S. market with the MSCI EAFE posting an 11.8% return, enhanced by a weak U.S. Dollar that contributed 7.0% of the quarterly return. On a YTD basis, MSCI EAFE returned 19.4% far outpacing the U.S. After a tough Q1, U.S. markets returned to growth with the S&P 500 10.9% return bringing the YTD return to 6.2%.

Economic indicators across Eurozone, UK and Asia continued to show mixed signals while dealing with an uncertain trade environment. From a sector perspective, Financials and Industrials led the MSCI EAFE higher during the quarter contributing over half the gain during 2Q. When drilling down into the industry groups, Banks and Capital Goods were the biggest individual contributors to the index performance. Bank performance has been driven by lower rates, a benign credit environment and little-to-no exposure to global trade tensions. On the other side of the ledger, the worst performing sectors were Energy, Health Care, and Consumer Discretionary. Energy was the only sector in the red with lower oil prices dragging. Turning to market factors, the Volatility, Momentum, and Growth factors were positive contributors during 2Q. Value, Yield and Size factors faced headwinds, while Quality factors had mixed results.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company International Equity portfolio returned 10.4% (10.2% net) during the quarter vs a return of 11.8% for the MSCI EAFE index. Stock selection was a headwind to performance, partially offset by positive sector exposure.

  • Our International Equity portfolio posted another quarter of strong absolute returns. While we slightly trailed the MSCI EAFE index, our relative results fell right in line with our 85-90% upside capture expectations. Similar to domestic markets, Quality and Lower Volatility factors underperformed across the developed international landscape, presenting headwinds to our portfolio.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Nintendo Co., Ltd ADR (7974 JP) (OTCPK:NTDOY)(OTCPK:NTDOF) – 7974 JP officially unveiled the Switch 2 in April, and the initial customer response has been strong. Early indicators such as pre-order numbers suggest demand for the console is ahead of management’s base case. We’re encouraged by these early results, and our 7974 JP thesis is on track.

  • BAE Systems PLC (BA/ LN) (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY)– BA/ LN, the leading UK defense contractor, performed strongly during 2Q. While continuing to report strong underlying financials results, widespread European and NATO government support for increased defense spending bolster investor sentiment and fundamentals. BA/ LN is positioned well to capitalize on future defense spending with the shares still at a reasonable valuation and conservative positioned balance sheet providing downside support.

  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd Sponsored ADR (TSM) – TSM, the leading semiconductor fabrication company, shares rebounded in 2Q after a tough start to the year. Fears over a slowdown in AI and data centers were largely overblown. TSM reported strong sales and profit performance throughout 2Q, exceeding expectations and confirming attractive structural demand fundamentals. We believe TSM still offers an attractive investment opportunity given the company’s dominant competitive position, favorable exposure to structural growth, and attractive valuation.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Willis Towers Watson, PLC (WTW) – WTW, a leading insurance broker, declined modestly in a strong upmarket after gaining 25% in the prior twelve months. The main reason for the pullback was due to fears of macro-driven weakness in the company’s discretionary consulting business within its Health, Wealth & Career (HWC) segment. We believe these fears are overblown as the recurring nature of much of WTW’s business positions the company to weather temporary, macro-driven challenges. The company has gained share and is on track to improve margins and free cash flow generation. Valuation is attractive and the balance sheet remains strong.

  • Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) – With the new CEO at the helm, management took the opportunity to air out APD’s remaining dirty laundry during the most recent earnings report. New management is on a path of de-risking these projects and returning APD to a higher structural level of profitability. We were encouraged to hear that free cash flow losses are expected to end after this year and a more astute capital allocation policy is being implemented. The strength and stability of the core industrial gas business remains unchanged.

  • Coloplast A/S (COLOB DC) (OTCPK:CLPBF)(OTCPK:CLPBY) – COLOB DC, a leading medical device company, has struggled with company-specific issues and wider industry level headwinds. During 2Q, the company’s CEO stepped down and was replaced on an interim basis by the Chairman who was the previous CEO. COLOB DC has the enviable position as a leader in continence, urology, ostomy, voice and respiratory care and wound care. These are critical product areas with structural demand growth and COLOB DC, for the most part, is the market leader. The company missed growth and margin targets as its Kerecis acquisition divided management attention. Under newly focused leadership, we believe COLOB DC will get back on track and at the current price represents an attractive opportunity.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight in both Energy and Health Care (weaker performing sectors)

  • What Hurt: Underweight in both Utilities and Financials (better performing sectors)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Sold: British American Tobacco PLC (BATS LN) (BTI) – BATS LN’s progress on its reduced-risk portfolio has underwhelmed and we decided to allocate the capital to opportunities where we had greater conviction.

  • Sold: AerCap Holdings (AER) – AER reached our estimate of fair value and we decided to allocate the capital to other opportunities.

  • Added: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd Sponsored ADR and ASML Holding NV (ASML NA) (ASML) – As our long-term conviction for both hasn’t changed, we took advantage of the share price weakness to add to our position.

  • Purchased: Intercontinental Hotels Group (IHG LN) (IHG) – IHG LN is a leading asset-light hotel franchisor with leading brands like Holiday Inn, Crown Plaza and InterContinental. As a franchisor, IHG LN is able to grow with little incremental invested capital allowing for management to return all its FCF to shareholders while still growing profits by double-digits annually. Management has proven to be astute and shareholder friendly. The balance sheet is under-leveraged providing downside protection. Lastly, the valuation is attractive as the market has gotten more concerned about an economic downturn hurting travel. We view the business as an attractive long-term investment.

Looking Ahead

From an economic perspective, we continue to expect trade negotiations to dominate headlines as they did in the first half of the year. In addition, current trends in monetary policy to continue with Europe and UK central banks to be in an accommodative posture while Japan is more likely to normalize rates higher if inflation proves sticky. Pro-growth fiscal policies in Europe and UK should be helpful to certain industries like construction, infrastructure and defense. The current backdrop around trade is uncertain and we’re prepared for volatility around upcoming trade negotiation deadlines. As we stated in our last update, we don’t rely on predicting or timing these macroeconomic outcomes. Longer-term, we operate with the expectation of modest economic growth with our focus at the individual company level.

In terms of equities, international markets have been boosted by the weak U.S. Dollar during the first half of the year and it’s difficult to predict future currency movements. We note that international markets are still valued at a discount to the U.S., despite the YTD market moves. We believe this will continue to be supportive of fund flows into international markets. With heightened risk, we believe it’s especially important to focus on quality factors and downside protection. Our portfolio is comprised of high-quality businesses run by good management teams with conservative balance sheets at attractive valuations that can survive and thrive through different market environments.

Benchmark Description: The MSCI EAFE Index (net) measures performance of large and mid-cap securities across developed markets around the world, including countries in Europe, Australia, Asia, and the Far East, excluding the U.S. and Canada. It includes over 900 securities, and covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each of the 21 countries. Benchmark returns are shown net of withholding taxes. Benchmark returns are not covered by the report of independent verifiers. Any referenced international index is presented net of foreign withholding tax, unless stated otherwise.

Performance and Fees: Gross of fee returns are calculated gross of management and custodian fees and net of transaction costs. data.title: Novo Nordisk: The Market Has Lost Its Mind
data.description:
Article Thesis

Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) reported its quarterly earnings results on Wednesday, Aug. 6th, showing ongoing appealing growth thanks to its weight-loss drug and its diabetes franchise. And yet, NVO's shares fell, following an already massive share price decline over the previous couple of months. Novo Nordisk is now a lot cheaper than it used to be, even before the weight-loss drug business started taking off, and its dividend yield is now (almost) at an all-time high. For a company with an excellent track record and a positive growth outlook, that does not seem logical, I believe—the market is underpricing NVO now, whereas it overpriced NVO during the peak of the Wegovy hype. I believe that buying now will pay off handsomely for long-term-oriented investors.

Past Coverage

I have written about Novo Nordisk several times here on Seeking Alpha in the past, which included Buy ratings before the weight-loss drug hype and a less bullish rating during the weight-loss drug hype. My most recent article is from this spring, when I gave Novo Nordisk a "Buy" rating. So far, this has not worked out, as shares continued to pull back. But I believe that the underlying growth and value story remains intact—NVO has gotten even cheaper. With Novo Nordisk announcing its Q2 results on Wednesday, it is time to take another look at this biopharma giant today.

What Happened?

On Wednesday morning, Novo Nordisk A/S reported its Q2 earnings results. These were the headline numbers:

    • Novo Nordisk press release (NVO): 1H GAAP EPADR of DKK 12.49.

    • Revenue of DKK 154.94B (+16.1% Y/Y).

Yes, growth was down compared to a year ago, when Novo Nordisk grew even more explosively. But despite growth slowing down to some extent, growth remained excellent, at 16%. Most biopharma companies, especially large ones like Novo Nordisk, grow not at all or only by a couple of percentage points. That's not surprising, as patent expirations, pressure to reduce drug costs, and the law of large numbers (nothing can grow at a very high pace forever) make it difficult to grow at a hefty pace beyond a certain point. And yet, NVO is delivering a mid-teens growth rate, whereas companies like Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) grow at a low single-digit pace only. I thus believe that a decline in NVO's growth rate shouldn't be overinterpreted—growth still is excellent on an absolute basis and on a relative basis, compared to other big pharma players, and it was clear that NVO would not grow at 20%+ forever. Let's take a deeper look at how the company has performed so far this year.

Novo Nordisk: Way More Than Just An Obesity Play

While Novo Nordisk has gotten a lot of attention for its obesity/weight-loss drug Wegovy in recent years, Novo Nordisk is much more than a single-drug company. Wegovy isn't even NVO's biggest drug, and weight management isn't Novo Nordisk's biggest franchise.

Instead, Novo Nordisk has, for many years, been focused on diabetes care. Here, the majority of NVO's revenues are generated, even today. NVO's diabetes business grew by 8% during the first half of the year, relative to one year earlier, adjusted for currency rate movements. This franchise includes GLP-1 drugs, mainly Ozempic, which showed appealing growth of 15%, as well as human insulin, long-lasting insulin, fast-acting insulin, and so on. Around two-thirds of Novo Nordisk's revenues are generated by its diabetes segment.

The next segment is what Novo Nordisk calls obesity care, which mainly consists of Wegovy, while Saxenda also contributes some revenues, although with a negative growth rate. Wegovy's strong growth has allowed the obesity care segment to grow by a very strong 58% compared to one year earlier during the first half of the current year, backing out currency rate movements.

Novo Nordisk's third segment is its rare diseases portfolio, which is smaller than the other two, but which is showing appealing growth as well: Revenues here were up by 15% so far this year, relative to one year earlier. Novo Nordisk's rare diseases business is not very large relative to the rest of the company, but it provides some nice diversification, and its growth is appealing (and not dependent on the rest of the company's portfolio).

While Novo Nordisk grew its revenues by 16% so far in 2025, its profit growth was considerably better. This is due to operating leverage, as Novo Nordisk's costs do not grow as fast as its revenues. Research and development costs, for example, are not tied to the revenues that the company generates and do not grow much when sales are rising. Operating leverage thus allows for margin expansion as the company grows, which is why Novo Nordisk's operating profit grew by a very nice 25% during a time when revenues rose by a still pretty nice, although not quite as nice, 16% (operating profit was up by 29% in constant currencies). A somewhat higher tax rate caused net income to grow a little less than operating profit, but net profits were up by 22% as well on a reported basis (and more on a currency-neutral basis).

While Novo Nordisk won't grow quite as much this year as it had thought at the beginning of the year, its outlook for 2025 is still pretty positive. The company guides for double-digit revenue growth, at around 11%, while operating profit is forecast to grow by 13%. The lower guidance, relative to half a year ago, is caused by competition for its Wegovy weight-loss drug stemming from compounded versions of this drug. But that's a problem that Novo Nordisk is tackling—the company has just started to move against compounders more intensely. The threat of lawsuits by a deep-pocketed company like Novo Nordisk should make at least some of the companies that compound Wegovy move away from this business model, and NVO may even get some court wins. While one can criticize management for ignoring this issue for too long, I like that the company is doing more to get rid of this competitive threat going forward, and I would not be surprised to see Wegovy's growth improve a couple of months ago as legal battles against drug compounders progress.

Beyond 2025, Novo Nordisk's growth outlook is positive as well. Analysts are forecasting revenue growth of around 10% in 2026, 2027, and 2028, which would be well above the industry average. Since Novo Nordisk is working on next-generation weight-loss drugs, such as CagriSema (which has recently shown very solid results in a trial), the obesity care segment could continue to grow at a nice pace for many years, I believe. Novo Nordisk also seeks to expand the usage of semaglutide, which is the active ingredient in Wegovy, in other areas, such as liver disease MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis). There are thus several growth vectors for Novo Nordisk, while its core diabetes care business should continue to deliver solid growth as well, thanks to a steadily growing market and NVO's excellent market position.

Before the recent hefty growth caused by NVO's success with Wegovy, Novo Nordisk grew by roughly 10% per year for many years, which seems achievable in the future, too. And yet, the market prices NVO as if its business were to perform way weaker in the future, relative to the past:

Novo Nordisk now trades at just 12x forward earnings, while it has, on average, traded at twice that valuation in the past. This would make sense if its growth over the coming years would be way weaker compared to the growth five or ten years ago, but that is, I believe, unlikely. While NVO will likely not grow by 20%-30% per year going forward, it didn't deliver such a growth rate during most of the past either. And yet, the market was willing to pay a 25x earnings multiple for a quality company growing at roughly 10% per year back then. Today, NVO trades at a way lower valuation, making it a pretty great value on a relative basis.

The very low valuation has also made NVO's dividend yield spike, as we can see in the following chart:

While investors oftentimes got a 1%-2% dividend yield when they bought in the past, they now get one of the highest yields ever—the dividend yield was only higher than today for a very short time during the initial COVID crash.

Is NVO A Good Investment?

Due to its strong position in the growing diabetes market, NVO has been a solid investment for many years. Its growth was not outrageous, but better than the big pharma average. I believe that this will hold true in the future, too, and many analysts agree, forecasting appealing business growth for NVO in 2025 and beyond.

Novo Nordisk now trades at less than one-third its price from a little more than a year ago, as the market has irrationally sold off NVO due to some temporary headwinds from drug compounding (NVO will still deliver double-digit growth this year). With NVO trading at less than half the long-term valuation average and with its shares now offering a pretty strong dividend yield, I believe it's a good time to buy into this quality company—the market is acting irrationally right now and offers a gift to long-term oriented investors.

data.title: VICI Properties: You Should At Least Consider The 5.1% Yield For Inflation Protection (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

With the market currently down over 500 points at the time of writing after reciprocal tariffs went into effect, the market is likely coming to grips with inflation potentially remaining higher for much longer.

And with more uncertainty likely in the coming months, buying into a company with a 5.1% yield doesn't sound like a bad idea, especially if of high quality.

With VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI), investors get a dividend yield over 5% that's well-covered to go along with more upside potential over the long-term. But due to their recent price appreciation, substantial upside may be limited near-term, reason for my downgrade from buy to a hold.

In this article I discuss VICI Properties' latest earnings, fundamentals, and why they could be still considered attractive.

Previous Thesis

I last covered VICI Properties back in May in an article titled: Near-Term Upside Likely Limited, But Still A Solid Bet Long-Term.

Since, VICI has performed well, holding up even better than I anticipated. The REIT is up a little over 6%, besting the S&P (SP500) who's up 5.78% over the same period.

I continued to rate VICI a buy due to their strong fundamentals, robust liquidity, and their then 5.5% dividend yield as I suspected they could hold up well during market volatility and increased economic uncertainty.

During Q1, VICI missed FFO estimates and declined from $0.57 to $0.51 the year prior. Revenue posted a solid beat by $7.17 million, amounting to $984.2 million.

Despite the FFO miss management raised full-year AFFO guidance. Their income stability and double-digit upside to their price target of $36.21 made them a solid bet long-term.

Strong Performance Amidst Uncertainty

As previously mentioned, VICI remains one of my core portfolio holdings, mainly for their income stability and upside potential. In their latest Q2 last week, VICI continued their strong performance despite a tough economic backdrop.

Revenue reached $1 billion, up from the previous quarter's $984.2 million, and 4.6% from the previous year's $957 million. AFFO also climbed $0.03 from the previous quarter to $0.60. Year-over-year, this represented a 4.9% increase on a per share basis.

In total, growth was slightly higher at 6.4%, AFFO growing from $592.4 million to $630.2 million. As a result of their performance, management again raised full-year AFFO guidance to $2.35 - $2.37, up from $2.33 - $2.36.This represents a 4.4% growth rate from 2024. Again, solid considering the macro environment.

Investment Activity

As far as investment activity, it was a fairly quiet quarter, but the REIT still found ways to produce capital through internal funding. And while some investors may worry about VICI providing funding to tenants, this has provided attractive returns for shareholders.

VICI's management touched on this during earnings:

To date in 2025, we are generating our earnings growth through a combination of same-store earnings growth and new store external growth. When it comes to same-store earnings growth, VICI's owners benefit from a same-store NOI growth rate that, according to Green Street's latest published net lease research is over 5x higher than the average projected rate of same-store NOI growth for net lease REITs. What you are seeing through this internally funded growth, are the advantages of VICI having achieved our current level of scale with more than $600 million in retained cash flow available for investment.

VICI provided a $510 million loan for the development of North Fork Mono, a casino & resort near Modera, California. The hotel is managed by Red Rock Resorts (RRR). This is part of the partnership the two companies announced back in May.

Moreover, with limited casinos in the state of California, the partnership opens up growth opportunities for both, particularly for VICI Properties. They also increased their previous investments in the One Beverly Hills development to $450 million, up from $300 million.

This is part of the previous announced partnership with Cain International and Eldridge Industries, which I touched on in my prior article. And going forward, I expect VICI to look for additional partnerships to drive future growth opportunities.

Balance Sheet

VICI also managed to deleverage the balance sheet, making good on their promise in the prior year's quarter. Net debt to EBITDA stood at 5.1x, down from 5.4x and within management's targeted range of 5.0x - 5.5x. Closing on their bond offering, the REIT has no debt maturing until the second half of 2026.

There's also a good chance of VICI refinancing at a lower rate as these both have weighted-average interest rates of 4.5% and 4.25% respectively.

Liquidity was plentiful with $621.5 million in forward equity after settling roughly 9.7 million shares for $296 million in net proceeds, which management used some to repay their maturing debt.

They also had $233 million in cash, bringing total liquidity to $2.9 billion. Total debt was $17.1 billion, basically flat year-over-year.

5.1% Yield Offers Inflation Protection

Although inflation has remained sticky, VICI's current 5.1% yield offers protection, likely the reason they've experienced upside in recent months and help up during volatility.

Moreover, with AFFO guidance raised on a per share and total basis, the dividend remains well-covered. The REIT should also be announcing a dividend increase soon which I'm expecting a new quarterly dividend between $0.45 - $0.46 a share. And you can see since inception, their dividend has been well-covered.

Management also mentioned dividends and inflation during earnings saying that dividend-paying stocks could rise in the future as investors look to income stocks as a result of sticky inflation risks and an aging population.

Additionally, while some readers discuss VICI's disappointing share price returns in recent years, their total returns since the beginning of 2019, the year after they IPO'd, is near identical to the S&P.

Limited Upside

Once interest rates come down and there's more clarity regarding the state of the economy, I expect VICI to break out, at least above the $35 level. With their new guidance, this gives VICI a forward P/AFFO multiple of 14.22x currently.

Using their normal multiple of 15.68x according to Fast Graphs, VICI's end of 2026 price target offers solid upside to their price target of $38.25. While I do think VICI deserves to trade at least around 15x - 16x, their upside will be highly dependent on the direction of interest rates.

Additionally, due to their recent price appreciation, investors should look to add to VICI on any share price weakness, particularly near the $30 level.

Risks

Although VICI has inflation protection in their annual rent escalators to mitigate some risks, the slowing economy which has impacted Las Vegas does have an impact on their operators.

If we continue to see the economy slow, their operators could run into financial issues, although I expect them to continue covering their full rent.

But this could result in potential downside or limited upside as a large portion of VICI's rent roll comes from the Las Vegas Strip with Caesars Entertainment (CZR) accounting for 39%.

Management also mentioned a slowdown in Canadian travel and gaming, but stated they were confident in the long-term trajectory of both Las Vegas and Canada.

Investor Takeaway

While VICI Properties may not see significant upside in the near term, the REIT still remans attractive due to their well-covered 5.1% yield and potential to see tailwinds from lower interest rates.

Additionally, their internal growth structure and size & scale has allowed them to grow earnings during heightened uncertainty, a testament to management and their fundamentals.

Moreover, with inflation likely to remain sticky due to tariffs, VICI's income reliability and price stability during a volatile market makes them an attractive option for income investors currently.

data.title: Embotelladora Andina Is Recovering And Fairly Valued
data.description:
Embotelladora Andina (NYSE:AKO.B) (NYSE:AKO.A) recently released its 2Q25 results.

I haven't reviewed the name in almost a year. Over that time, the company's business has improved, mainly in Argentina, and in part, following the dynamic of the Chilean stock market, the share has appreciated more than 20%.

In this article, I use a different approach to value the name, not based on simple earnings but also on available cash flow and growth. I find that the name can provide a return of about 11/12%, not particularly attractive given the cyclical position, and therefore maintain a Hold rating.

Coming from a challenging place

Last time I wrote about Andina, the company was facing challenges in most of the countries where it operates.

In particular, in Argentina, the economic adjustment carried by the new government led to a tremendous drop in volumes and profitability, which is only moderately being recovered so far. In Brazil and Chile, persistent inflation and high rates moderate the pace of income growth and pricing power, and in all countries, depreciation of the currency impacted on costs.

Some of those factors have reverted in FY25 YTD, as evidenced in the company's results.

Argentina's better, albeit expensive

Starting with Argentina, sales have improved significantly, almost 25% in 2Q25 and YTD. 2Q24 was probably the worst point of the economic adjustment, and therefore, the cyclical recovery is stronger. During the call (so far only in Spanish in audio format), management said that the company has already recovered 75% of the ground lost in 2024, and that going forward, the recovery will be more moderate.

In fact, it was Argentina the driver of most of the revenue growth of the company in the quarter, and of all of its profitability. When we compare CLP revenues and EBITDA, 60% of the sales increase (CLP 70 billion) came from Argentina (CLP 40 billion), and the totality of EBITDA (CLP 10 billion).

When talking of CLP figures, though, and as I always comment on any foreign company operating in Argentina, we need to be sure to consider that Argentina is becoming more expensive in USD and CLP terms because inflation is running hotter than the rate of depreciation. That means Argentina becomes more profitable in units and aggregate terms. The problem, as always with Argentina, is that these dynamics are seldom sustainable, and usually the country goes from very expensive to very cheap in foreign currency.

That means, in my opinion, that the aggregate profitability of the country is probably a little lower, albeit estimating how much is hard. Argentina's multi-lateral real exchange rate had appreciated by 20% between 2Q24 and 2Q25. Part of that has already reverted in 3Q25.

More pricing power in Chile and Brazil, albeit depreciated

In Brazil and Chile, the company recovered pricing capacity, being able to grow revenues above volumes in local currency, and passing some of the effect of inflation (MSD in both countries) to consumers.

The offset of this was higher costs coming from depreciated currencies.

Some of the company's largest costs are dollar-denominated, namely the concentrate bought from Coca-Cola (KO), sugar (outside of Brazil), and PET resins used for the bottling plants.

Coca-Cola, in part, offsets the effect of depreciation on the price of concentrate. During the call, some of this was discussed around the distribution agreement. We can also note in the results the comment that concentrate prices decrease,d offsetting the depreciation of the local currencies.

In Brazil, volumes were up only 1% but sales were up 9% in local currency, because of pricing power capacity. However, costs were up 13% because of the depreciation, and therefore, BRL's profitability fell.

In Chile, volumes were better, at 3.5%, and pricing added another 5 percentage points of growth in sales. In this case, costs were in line with sales, at 8.5%.

A cold year

In all of the company's markets, winter has been significantly colder than in previous years. This obviously impacts the consumption of cold beverages, which are the company's staples. This had an impact in 2Q25, and will probably have an impact in 3Q25 (which includes the winter months of July and August in the southern Hemisphere).

The company did not provide a figure for this impact, though, but we can expect that under other conditions, sales would have been even better.

No big changes ahead

All of this talks of a context in which demand, albeit not booming, has recovered.

The company does not expect a significant improvement or worsening in any of its markets. It expects LSD growth in Chile through this year (which is electoral), with little volatility after the change of government, considering the relatively conservative political outlook in Chile. In Brazil, the company expects that conditions will remain positive for consumer products, as the government puts a focus on poverty alleviation. Finally, in Argentina, it expects continued recover,y albeit more moderate. There were no comments on the call about the FX reversal risk.

A cash and growth valuation model

Throughout all the time I have been covering Andina (first article in 2021), I have used a P/E valuation approach, focusing on the long-term profitability of the company.

Although this method is valid, I want to delve one step deeper in this article, and separate earnings from cash available for shareholder returns (dividends), and also account for growth potential.

Starting with profitability across the cycle, I believe using an average of 12% in EBIT margins is conservative, instead of using the current figures, where profitability margins are higher, mainly because of Argentina's higher productivity.

At 12% EBIT margin and $3.5 billion in revenues, we can expect the company to generate EBIT of about $420 million across the cycle. From this, we need to remove approximately $50 million in interest expenses and 30% in taxes to reach $260 million in earnings.

So far, there's no difference from a classical P/E approach. However, in order to add growth and actual returns, we need a few more assumptions.

On the returns side, I expect that the company's leverage (assets to equity) levels will remain more or less constant, and that asset utilization will not continue to grow. This will allow me to assume that the company will require to finance growth in equity (CAPEX above D&A and working capital) in line with earnings growth.

For growth, one simplifying assumptions are flat margins over time. This is logical after assuming a cycle-average level for EBIT margin, and a consistent leverage level (given that debt has to move up with equity to keep leverage constant).

Another assumption is that the P/E ratio remains constant. This is needed for earnings growth to become a shareholder return via share appreciation. We could assume (bullishly) that the multiple can expand, but I prefer to remain conservative.

Finally, I believe the name can grow revenues in USD at about 5% over the long-term, this is based in moderate LSD growth in the countries where it operates, plus another LSD addition from inflation over time globally.

Based on this, therefore, we get a yield of 5% only from the growth. From earnings ($260 million), we need to remove the cost of growth, that is, 5% of equity ($1.1 billion, or $55 million). That means the company would have about $205 million to distribute to shareholders. Indeed, this figure is consistent with long-term payout ratios.

Finally, we need the market cap. Embotelladora has two classes of shares, and in most databases, the company therefore shows two different market caps. However, based on the share data (page 18 of the recent release), we know that there are 76.5 million ADS of each class, meaning an actual market cap of about $3.3 billion.

This means the company offers a potential cash flow distributable yield of 6.6%, plus growth of 5%. This is a total of about 11/12%.

However, the shares have different rights; the B shares receive 10% more dividends than the A shares. After some equations, we can therefore find that B shares have rights to $97.5 million ($1.28 per ADS) if the $205 million were paid as dividends, and the B shares have rights to $107.5 million ($1.4 per ADS).

That means the A shares have a potential dividend yield of 7% and the B shares one of 6%. The A shares are therefore slightly more attractive at this valuation.

I think, however, that 11% or 12% are not particularly attractive for a name exposed to emerging economies, with the potential for a tactical reversal in earnings in one of its largest markets (Argentina), and under the risk of earnings depreciation (in USD).

I believe the name is fairly priced, albeit not a steal. I therefore maintain a Hold rating on AKO
